coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-driven handheld monitoring of Cr(VI) and living cell imaging using nitrogen-doped yellow fluorescence carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Liru
Zhang, Bianxiang
Zhang, Caihong
Zhang, Yan
Shuang, Shaomin
Shi, Lihong
coredata.dc:description: User-friendly, field-portable, and direct read-out mini-device is indispensable for under-developed countries or resources-insufficient areas. Herein, a field-portable handheld monitoring platform incorporating smartphone, 3D-printed device, and test strip stained with yellow fluorescence (FL) carbon dots (YCDs) proves the efficacy for real-time quantitation of Cr(VI) by a consecutive FL color transformation. YCDs were synthesized with starch, rhodamine 6G, and L-lysine through hydrothermal procedure, manifesting intriguing yellow-emitting behavior locating at 556 nm under 470 nm excitation. With increasing Cr(VI) concentration, FL intensity of YCDs at 556 nm is significantly quenched, accompanying by a continuous FL colour alteration from bright yellow to dark yellow. Inspired by above FL phenomena, YCDs-based test strips are fabricated to realize point-of-care quantifying of Cr(VI). Inspiringly, developed field-portable handheld monitoring platform is further generalized to on-site quantify Cr(VI) concentration in tap water based on wide linear ranges of 0–800 μM, and low limit of detection of 0.349 μM. Furthermore, laser confocal imaging of HeLa cells corroborates that YCDs can be utilized for visual determination of Cr(VI) in living cells, illustrating that YCDs presents powerful biosensing application prospect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Handheld determination
Biosensing
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Yellow fluorescence
Cr(VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of field-portable handheld monitoring platform
Determination of Cr(VI) by field-portable handheld monitoring platform
Discrimination of Cr(VI) in watery solutions
Identifying of Cr(VI) in living cells
Field-portable handheld monitoring platform for real-time quantifying of Cr(VI) in tap water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Construction and inner structure of field-portable handheld monitoring platform. (B) Detection and analysis process of field-portable handheld monitoring platform.

(A–C) YCDs’ TEM pictures at scale bars of 50 nm, 20 nm, and 10 nm, respectively. (Inset of Fig. 2A is YCDs’ diameter distribution). (D) YCDs’ AFM topographic picture. (Inset of Fig. 2D is radial height distribution map through statistical analysis of YCDs on white line). (E) YCDs’ AFM 3D picture. (F) YCDs’ height distribution. (G) YCDs’ XPS full-scan spectrum. (H–J) C1s, O1s, and N1s spectra of YCDs. (K) YCDs’ FTIR spectrum.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line), FL excitation spectrum (blue line) and emission spectrum (red line) of YCDs. (B) FL spectra of YCDs at various excitation wavelengths (420 nm, 430 nm, 440 nm, 450 nm, 460 nm, 470 nm, 480 nm, and 490 nm). (C) Calculated CIE coordinate from YCDs’ FL spectrum. (Inset: photos of YCDs under 470 nm irradiation (left) and daylight (right)). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A–C) Selectivity of YCDs for Cr(VI) at pH = 7.0 under 470 nm excitation conditions. Yellow bars: introducing various ions and amino acids to YCDs system, grey bars: subsequently adding Cr(VI) to above-mentioned solution. (F and F0 are the FL intensity of YCDs in the existence and absence of external substance, separately). (D) YCDs’ FL spectra with introducing diverse Cr(VI) amount (0–800 μM) at 470 nm excitation. (E) Relationship between Ln(F/F0 + 0.048) and Cr(VI) amounts (0–800 μM), and (F) corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram for Cr(VI) discrimination (Inset: colour shifts trend of YCDs solution). (G) Zeta potentials of YCDs and YCDs + Cr(VI). (H) UV–vis spectra of YCDs as increasing Cr(VI) amount (0–800 μM). (I) Plot of (1/(F0-F)) versus Cr(VI) concentrations (1/[(Cr(VI)]) (0–800 μM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A–B) Photos of YCDs paper strips with various cations (up to 800 μM) and diverse amounts of Cr(VI) (0–800 μM) at UV light. (C) Linearity correlation between V/S value and Cr(VI) amount (0–800 μM).
LSCM imaging of HeLa cells incubated with YCDs + Cr(VI) under 405 nm excitation (λem = 500–700 nm).
Schematic diagram of preparation as well as applications of YCDs.
Field-portable handheld monitoring platform for real-time quantifying of Cr(VI) in tap water.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-driven handheld monitoring of Cr(VI) and living cell imaging using nitrogen-doped yellow fluorescence carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: User-friendly, field-portable, and direct read-out mini-device is indispensable for under-developed countries or resources-insufficient areas. Herein, a field-portable handheld monitoring platform incorporating smartphone, 3D-printed device, and test strip stained with yellow fluorescence (FL) carbon dots (YCDs) proves the efficacy for real-time quantitation of Cr(VI) by a consecutive FL color transformation. YCDs were synthesized with starch, rhodamine 6G, and L-lysine through hydrothermal procedure, manifesting intriguing yellow-emitting behavior locating at 556 nm under 470 nm excitation. With increasing Cr(VI) concentration, FL intensity of YCDs at 556 nm is significantly quenched, accompanying by a continuous FL colour alteration from bright yellow to dark yellow. Inspired by above FL phenomena, YCDs-based test strips are fabricated to realize point-of-care quantifying of Cr(VI). Inspiringly, developed field-portable handheld monitoring platform is further generalized to on-site quantify Cr(VI) concentration in tap water based on wide linear ranges of 0–800 μM, and low limit of detection of 0.349 μM. Furthermore, laser confocal imaging of HeLa cells corroborates that YCDs can be utilized for visual determination of Cr(VI) in living cells, illustrating that YCDs presents powerful biosensing application prospect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of rapid response and portability, fluorescence (FL) paper sensor has been extensively utilized for visual determination of various analytes such as metal ions [1–4], explosives [5,6], and pesticides [7]. Nonetheless, it fails to realize satisfactory sensitivity and accuracy owing to its high dependence on naked eyes with poor capability of discriminating a slight color change. By virtue of high-quality camera and built-in color identification application (APP), smartphone combined with FL paper sensor can make visual analysis possible. In recent years, such smartphone sensing platform has been constructed for quantitative monitoring. Monisha et al. designed silver nanoparticles for quantitative determination of mercuric ion utilizing the smartphone sensing platform [8]. Nadi et al. exploited mango leaf extract-stained probe for identifying ammonia in aquaculture wastewater employing the platform [9]. Arsawiset et al. detected formaldehyde and hydrogen sulfide through coupling 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane with chromogenic substrates adopting platform [10] . Amalraj et al. mixed Co(II) and bi-organic ligand to analyze phosphate and copper ions via the platform [11]. Feng et al. exploited tri-site FL sensor for visualization and quantitation analysis of H2S using the platform [12]. However, for the smartphone sensing platform, the most frequently encountered challenge is the inconsistency of recorded images or photos under different external conditions.

To provide a consistent detection environment, 3D-printing has been employed to fabricate an enclosed chamber with built-in UV light and test strip slot. By integrating smartphone, 3D-printed dark cavity, and test strip, a field-portable handheld monitoring platform can be constructed for real-time visual quantitation. Currently, the field-portable handheld monitoring platform has greatly piqued researchers’ interest. Mei et al. employed Cu2+ decorated NaYF4:Yb/Tm nansensor for on-site handheld detecting of pesticide thiram [13]. Li et al. constructed a silicon doped carbon dots (CDs)-Fe3+ FL probe for in-situ determination of ascorbic acid by means of the field-portable platform [14]. Cheng et al. synthesized a red-FL europium complex sensor for the on-site handheld quantitation of mancozeb [15]. Yan et al. fabricated lanthanide metal–organic framework Eu(TATB) for real-time quantitation analysis of sulfonamide via handheld platform [16]. Liu et al. developed coumarin-based FL sensor for in-field handheld identifying Hg2+ [17]. Nevertheless, FL sensors for above-mentioned field-portable handheld monitoring platform have some disadvantages such as costly precursors, cumbersome preparation procedure, as well as high toxicity. Consequently, for the field-portable handheld monitoring platform, there is an exigent demand to excavate chemical probes with merits of low cost, easy-to-prepare, and low toxicity.

CDs have gradually become superior alternatives to traditional FL materials owing to their fantastic photoluminescence properties, fabulous photostability, low cost, easy-to-prepare, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [18–23]. These distinct benefits endow CDs with practical application potential in FL sensing, catalysis, bioimaging, fingerprint detection, and nanomedicine [24–31]. In FL sensing, several possible sensing mechanisms for FL changes of CDs have been proposed. Bavya et al. developed aggregation-induced blue-FL enhanced CDs for identifying of Hg(II) [32]. Yang et al. exploited green FL CDs and Au nanoparticles for carbendazim determination through FL resonance energy transfer [33]. Zhang et al. developed CDs of FL quenching for the determination of methyl parathion stem from inner filter effect [34]. Nevertheless, aboved-mentioned CDs are generally in the short-wavelength domain, which may be interfered from cell autofluorescence and bring about photodamage toward tissues in biological application. Thus, the design of CDs-based sensors with long-wavelength emission is particularly desirable.

Herein, adopting field-portable handheld monitoring platform, amylum-based yellow FL CDs (YCDs) sensors have been ingeniously designed to visually quantify Cr(VI) (Scheme 1 ). Cr(VI) is an highly toxic element for organisms, which may render enzymes and proteins inactive and cause harmful for human body [35]. Additionally, excessive intake of Cr(VI) in human tissues could bring about anemia, cancer, respiratory and digestive diseases. Therefore, the detecting of Cr(VI) is highly significant toward human health and life [36–38]. In present study, YCDs-based sensors were fabricated with starch, rhodamine 6G, and L-lysine through hydrothermal method. Intriguingly, yellow FL of CDs was distinctly quenched upon introduction of Cr(VI), accompanying by a continuous FL color diversification of bright yellow to dark yellow. Based on above obvious FL color variations, YCDs test strips are fabricated to facilitate field-portable identifying of Cr(VI). Besides, YCDs paper strips could be utilized to precisely in-field analyze Cr(VI) via the value/saturation (V/S) values gained from a color recognizer application employing the field-portable handheld monitoring platform. Furthermore, YCDs can be generalized as a visual biosensor to track intracellular Cr(VI).

Starch (0.2 g), rhodamine 6G (0.2 g), and L-lysine (0.2 g) as raw materials were added in ultrapure water (30 mL), and the aboved-mixture was poured into 50 mL hydrothermal reactor vessel, then reacted at 200℃ for 4h. After the hydrothermal reactor vessel cooling down naturally, obtained turbid liquid were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min, subsequently filtered using 0.22 μm microporous membrane, then dialyzed against DDI water through a dialysis membrane with MWCO of 500–1000 Da for 24 h. Ultimately, as-prepared YCDs solution was collected and preserved in a refrigerator.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was implemented in the JEOL JEM-2100 (Tokyo, Japan) instrument working under the accelerating voltage of 200 KV. Atomic force microscope (AFM) pictures were gained through the AFM Bruker MultiMode 8 in the contact mode. UV–vis absorption spectrum was finished through the Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescent spectra were finished using the F-280 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Tianjin, China). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) data were gained by the AXISULTRA DLD electron spectrometer. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained by Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR Spectrometer (US). All FL pictures were recorded using Zeiss LSM880 laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM).

The sensitivity responses of YCDs to Cr(VI) was implemented employing a phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution (pH = 7.0). Firstly, 50 μL YCDs stock solution is diluted in 500 μL PBS buffer. Subsequently, various contents of Cr(VI) solution were separately introduced.

Selectivity determination of YCDs toward Cr(VI) was appraised through adding of typical ions and anions (up to 800 μM), including Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cr(VI), Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Br−, CH3COO−, Cl−, CO3 2−, F−, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −, PO4 3−, S2−, S2O3 2−, SCN−, SO4 2−, alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (Glu), glycine (Gly), isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), threonine (Thr), and tyrosine (Tyr). At the same conditions as above previously, the interference experiment was explored under the Cr(VI)/interfering ions concentration ratio of 1. The FL spectra were finished under room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of field-portable handheld monitoring platform
ce:section.ce:para: Field-portable handheld monitoring platform incorporates smartphone, 3D-printed handheld device, and YCDs-stained test strip. 3D model of the handheld device (Fig. 1 A) was designed by 3D modeling software, and then corresponding specifications were imported into It-600 printer to acquire 3D-printed handheld device (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) by field-portable handheld monitoring platform
ce:section.ce:para: Determination of Cr(VI) was implemented through field-portable handheld monitoring platform (Fig. 1B). Smartphone installed Color Picker application was applied to capture color of test strip as well as output the corresponding hue-saturation-value (HSV) values. Furthermore, quantification identifying is accomplished through building standard curve between color signal value and concentration of intended analytes.

Firstly, cellulose-based filter paper (diameter of 11 cm) was adopted and YCDs solution is immobilized on it. Then, the filter paper was removed from aboved-solution and dried in the air after 2 min. Subsequently, various metal ions were dropped on the YCDs-stained and air-dried at room temperature. The variation of the FL color on the test strips was studied through field-portable handheld monitoring platform.

TEM pictures (Fig. 2 A–C) reveal that YCDs possess a pseudo-spherical appearance and uniform dispersion. The size distribution range of YCDs mostly is 2.8–5.6 nm with even particle size of 4.2 ± 0.70 nm (inset of Fig. 2A). Additionally, AFM images displayed in Fig. 2D–2E intuitively demonstrate that YCDs have excellent dispersity. Inset of Fig. 2D presents the radial height distribution profile through following the white line. The height scope of YCDs is calculated to be 2.4–3.2 nm with a mean height of 2.8 nm (Fig. 2F), having a great consistent with TEM findings.

XPS was performed to explore YCDs’ elemental composition. Full-scan spectrum of YCDs (Fig. 2G) illustrates that as-prepared YCDs exists three elements locating at 283.4 eV, 531.3 eV, and 400.6 eV, attributing to C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively. C1s spectrum (Fig. 2H) is separated into three peaks, containing CC (284.8 eV), C–O (286.4 eV), and CO (287.9 eV). High-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2I) is differentiated as two bands locating at 531.9 eV and 533.6 eV, implying the existence of CO and C–OH/O–C–O. Deconvolution of N1s signal (Fig. 2J) is disassembled into two peaks of C–N and N–H, which are centered at 399.6 eV and 402.2 eV, respectively. The functional groups of the obtained YCDs were also investigated through FTIR displayed in Fig. 2K. The broad band locating at 3315.9 cm−1 demonstrates the existence of O–H bond. The peaks centered at 3043.2 cm−1 and 2935.9 cm−1 arise from presence of N–H and C–H bond, respectively. The sharp enhanced peak at 2063.9 cm−1 corresponds to CO bond, and the broad band centered at 1566.1 cm−1 and 824.2 cm−1 are associated with N–H bending vibration. The absorption peaks locating at 1393.9 cm−1 and 1142.2 cm−1 confirm the C–N and C–O–C stretching vibration. The sharp enhanced band at 1017.5 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of C–O, and the absorption peak centered at 931.1 cm−1 is attributed to bending vibration of C–H. Consistency findings with XPS are acquired, and thus corroborating that surface of YCDs contains hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups.

UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 A) presents two sharp peaks locating at 270.8 nm and 512.8 nm, which may derive from the π − π* transition of core state as well as n − π* transition of surface states, respectively. Through transforming YCDs’ excitation wavelengths from 420 nm to 490 nm (Fig. 3B), FL intensity of YCDs first increases and then decreases. The excitation-independent emission is attributed to the existence of surface state [39,40]. Fig. 3B demonstrates a total consistency with YCDs’ excitation-emission spectra of Fig. 3A, suggesting that YCDs’ optimal emission wavelengths are 556 nm at 470 nm optimal excitation. Besides, solution of YCDs emits orange under sunlight and bright yellow FL under 470 nm excitation (Fig. 3C). Furthermore, FL color of YCDs is further calculated using the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3C), which is coincident with the color of YCDs solution under 470 nm excitation (inset of Fig. 3C). Additionally, calculated relative quantum yield of YCDs are as high as 30.7 % adopting rhodamine 6G as a reference.

FL stability of YCDs was studied under various external stimuli and acid-base conditions for investigating their practical application. As demonstrated in Fig. S2A, different concentrations of NaCl (0–1 M) illustrates minimal fluctuations on FL intensity of YCDs. Additionally, FL intensity of YCDs maintains constant for 45 min continuous irradiation as well as 20 days storage conditions (Fig. S2B–C). Fig. S2D illustrates that FL intensity of YCDs remains constant under acid-base condition with pH values from 3 to 12. Above phenomena reveal prominent optical properties of YCDs, laying the foundation for potential application of YCDs in complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of Cr(VI) in watery solutions
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate YCDs’ selectivity, the influences of various ions and amino acids (up to 800 μM) on FL intensity were investigate at pH = 7.0 under 470 nm excitation, including Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cr(VI), Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Br−, CH3COO−, Cl−, CO3 2−, F−, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −, PO4 3−, S2−, S2O3 2−, SCN−, SO4 2−, Ala, Arg, Asp, Cys, Glu, Gly, Ile, Leu, Lys, Phe, Pro, Thr, and Tyr. Only Cr(VI) displays the largest quenching for FL intensity ratio (F/F0), while the analogous phenomenon does not appear upon introducing other analytes (Fig. 4 A–C), testifying admirable specificity of YCDs toward Cr(VI). Furthermore, the existence of interfering substances (up to 800 μM) exhibits negligible influence on the determination of Cr(VI) (Fig. 4A–C).

YCDs’ monitoring ability for Cr(VI) was studied upon pH = 7.0 at 470 nm excitation. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D, yellow peak of YCDs at 556 nm decreases significantly with increasing of Cr(VI) concentrations. Notably, there exists a fantastic linear relationship through plotting Ln(F/F0 + 0.048) and Cr(VI) contents (varying from 0 to 800 μM) (Fig. 4E). Besides, An extremely low limit of detection (LOD) of 7.5 nm is determined following to signal/noise ratio of 3 and is evidently lower than previous reports for detecting Cr(VI) (Table S1), suggesting that proposed Cr(VI) sensor has superior sensitivity. Moreover, FL color changes trend of YCDs at various Cr(VI) concentrations is calculated accurately using CIE chromaticity diagram displayed in Fig. 4F, which corroborates an excellent agreement with FL colour of YCDs solution presented in inset of Fig. 4F. These results demonstrate the feasibleness of YCDs for visual discrimination Cr(VI).

The response mechanism of YCDs toward Cr(VI) is further investigated. The surface of YCDs is covered by –NH2, –COOH and –OH. Upon adding Cr(VI) to YCDs solution, Cr(VI) is easily reacted with –NH2 on the surface of YCDs to form new non-FL YCDs@Cr(VI) complex [41,42]. To prove the conjecture, zeta potential as well as UV–vis absorption of YCDs in the presence of Cr(VI) were implemented. After adding Cr(VI), zeta potential of YCDs (Fig. 4G) increases from –11.1 mV to –4.5 mV, probably attributing to the electrostatic interaction between YCDs and Cr(VI). Additionally, there emerges a new absorption band at 356.8 nm in UV–vis spectrum, which may result from the reaction between Cr(VI) and YCDs to form a new non-FL YCDs@Cr(VI) compound based on electrostatic interaction (Fig. 4H).

Besides, the absorption intensity of YCDs gradually enhances with increasing Cr(VI) concentrations, indicating static quenching process originated from the formation of the non-FL YCDs@Cr(VI) compound. In addition, Fig. 4I presented good linear relationship of (1/(F0-F)) versus (1/[(Cr(VI)]) (R2 = 0.989) which fits Lineweaver-Burk equation and shows the static quenching mechanism. Above results demonstrate that the sensing mechanism of YCDs for Cr(VI) is static quenching.

By virtue of the continuous colour transformation of YCDs system in the reaction, field-portable handheld monitoring platform is established for rapid quantification of Cr(VI). Selectivity of YCDs test strip for Cr(VI) was determined through introducing diverse cations on test strip. Only Cr(VI) makes yellow FL of YCDs test strip significantly fade under UV light, while the similar phenomenon fails to occur after adding other competitive substances, revealing high selectivity of YCDs test strip for Cr(VI) (Fig. 5 A). Additionly, sensitivity of YCDs test strip toward Cr(VI) was evaluated using field-portable handheld monitoring platform. YCDs test strips exhibit successive FL colour variation from bright yellow to dark yellow when addition of various concentrations of Cr(VI) on test strips (Fig. 5B). Interestingly, FL color of YCDs-based test strips can be shifted into HSV values via a colour identification application built-in smartphone. And a fantastic linear correlation with R2 = 0.992 is exhibited through plotting V/S values and Cr(VI) concentrations (varying from 0 to 800 μM), and LOD of 0.349 μM is gained. Consistent results with FL spectrophotometry analysis are obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identifying of Cr(VI) in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Biosensing ability of YCDs was further evaluated through studying Cr(VI)-responsive bioimaging in HeLa cells. The cellular cytotoxicity of YCDs to HeLa cells was initially inspected. As displayed in Fig. S3, cell survival rate reaches above 95 % even at 5 mg mL−1 of YCDs, illustrating low cytotoxicity of YCDs on HeLa cells. Subsequently, YCDs-stained HeLa cells are further tracked under 405 nm laser excitation through capturing yellow FL channel (λem = 500–700 nm). It can be evidently noticed that YCDs-stained HeLa cells emit bright yellow FL. Upon the introduction of Cr(VI), FL brightness of HeLa cells exhibits the obvious quenching with extended time (Fig. 6 ). Such findings firmly corroborated that YCDs can be generalized as biosensors for monitoring Cr(VI) in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field-portable handheld monitoring platform for real-time quantifying of Cr(VI) in tap water
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the practicality of the proposed handheld monitoring protocol, standard recovery assays were carried out in pretreated tap water. Analysis findings of Cr(VI) in tap water utilizing field-portable handheld monitoring platform are presented in Table 1 . When Cr(VI) concentrations are 50, 150, 250, 350, and 450 μM in tap water, the recoveries are calculated to be 103.78 %, 101.08 %, 98.96 %, 100.61 %, and 99.96 %, respectively. Besides, the relative standard deviations (RSD) are found within 0.14 %–0.66 %. These results corroborate the great application prospects of proposed strategy for Cr(VI) discrimination in the field of environmental monitoring.

Nitrogen-doped YCDs have been intelligently fabricated through one-pot hydrothermal procedure. Obtained YCDs have been successfully employed as ideal FL sensors to measure Cr(VI) in aqueous solution (0–800 μM). Concomitantly, a successive FL color transformation from bright yellow to dark yellow lays a foundation for visualizing detection of Cr(VI) through field-portable handheld monitoring platform integrating YCDs test strip, smartphone, and 3D-printed handheld device. Additionally, YCDs test strip is first utilized for on-site quantify Cr(VI) in tap water by the proposed platform. Furthermore, as-synthesized YCDs can be generalized as biosensor for visualize tracking Cr(VI) (800 μM) in living cells using LSCM. It is fervently desired that proposed methodologies possess bright application prospect in on-site quantification and biological sensing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Bianxiang Zhang reports was provided by Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project (No. 202202040201009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126429.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Barrier-free patterned paper sensors for multiplexed heavy metal detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, L. Guan, Z. Lu, M. Li, J. Wu, R. Cao, J. Tian, Barrier-free patterned paper sensors for multiplexed heavy metal detection, Talanta 196 (2019) 408–414, doi: 10.1016/j.talanta.2018.12.096.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of cyclodextrin functionalized photoluminescent metal nanoclusters for chemoselective Fe3+ ion detection in aqueous medium and its applications of paper sensors and cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mohandoss, S. Palanisamy, S.G. You, Y. R. Lee, Synthesis of cyclodextrin functionalized photoluminescent metal nanoclusters for chemoselective Fe3+ ion detection in aqueous medium and its applications of paper sensors and cell imaging, J. Mol. Liq. 356 (2022) 118999−119010, doi: 10.1016/j.molliq.2022.118999.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation-independent yellow-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for biological imaging and paper-based sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Shi, L. Li, X. Li, G. Zhang, Y. Zhang, C. Dong, S. Shuang, Excitation-independent yellow-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for biological imaging and paper-based sensing, Sensor Actuat. B-Chem. 251 (2017) 234–241, doi: 10.1016/j.snb.2017.05.065.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of ZnO@ZnS core–shell nanoparticles for detection of lead and iron ions in aqueous solutions by colorimetric paper sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. A. Bakhtiari, H. Abdoos, F. Karimzadeh, Green synthesis of ZnO@ZnS core–shell nanoparticles for detection of lead and iron ions in aqueous solutions by colorimetric paper sensors, Chem. Pap. 76 (2022) 99–109, doi: 10.1007/s11696-021-01840-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recyclable fluorescent paper sensor for visual detection of nitroaromatic explosives

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly Selective and sensitive fluorescent paper sensor for nitroaromatic explosive detection
